It is unclear how much of an impact he made in America. He actually did break another record for the time, as he performed 86 lectures (most of them in English) within a few months, practically one per day across the entire continent. Upon his return from America, van Gennep fell ill. The doors into French academia seemed closed and locked. He could see no alternatives. He was failing to make an impact. And he quite simply could not get a job. At that point he decided to give up on all his academic ambitions, and settled down with his wife as a chicken-breeder in a farm-place in Southern France. Luckily for the fate of French and European folklore, his questioning mind could not rest, and within a year he returned with his wife to Bourg-la-Reine – and to his writings. This is exactly the period when we can observe a decisive turn to French folklore, and it is likely a decision that matured while taking care of his chickens. It was this discipline that he more or less single-handedly built up over the next decades, working on his own, and without payment, now again making his living from translations and editorial work – but that is another story, beyond the thresholds of this book, and this is where our narrative breaks off. The turn to folklore was not an abandonment of anthropology and sociology, for van Gennep never perceived these to be entirely different disciplines. Rather, it was probably a realistic calculation that this was the only way in which he could be allowed to make at least a limited impact, and maybe even the only way to get published in French. Without ever holding an academic position in France, van Gennep would become known as the father of French folklore, but his relevance for anthropology and sociology was by then practically annulled. It should be said that Arnold van Gennep’s name did not disappear entirely from the radar. There are some indications that Mauss continued to hold him in regard. In the 1920s Mauss and his collaborators finally got the Institut d’Ethnologie established. For the first batch of students, the Institute, next to the regular courses (more than half of which were taught by Mauss on ‘Instructions in Descriptive page 68, with these sympathetic lines: ‘Notre Collègue et Ami, M. Arnold van Gennep a soutenu le 24 janvier dernier ses thèses en Sorbonne pour l’obtention du doctorat ès Lettres devant MM. Lévy-Bruhl, Loisy, Guignebert, Bouglé, Brunschwicg et Lods. Sujets : 1° De l’État actuel du Problème totèmique (avec discussion des théories du regretté Déchelette sur le totémisme préhistorique) ; 2° Rites de Passage, parmi lesquels ceux qui comportent un passage sous un dolmen ou tout autre monument préhistorique sont bien connus de nos collègues’. Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 43 Ethnography’), offered a series of four ‘open lectures’. Two of those open lectures were taught by Arnold van Gennep, on the ‘geographical method of folklore’ (Fournier 2006: 238). It is the only direct testimony we actually have of Arnold van Gennep teaching in France. The very fact that he was here narrowly identified with the method of folklore is more than indicative. He would not become part of the permanent teaching staff. From the mid-1920s, van Gennep concentrated on folklore and lived entirely from his freelance income. It was just enough to sustain his little family, which lived in very humble circumstances. This was the situation until 1945, where the French Centre for Scientific Research (CNSR) awarded him a grant that enabled him to work full-time on his Manuel de Folklore Français Contemporain. Incredibly enough, van Gennep was 72 years old before he received his first formal recognition within the French state. He dedicated most of his old age energies to his multi-volume work on French folklore, making huge advances, and compiling nine thick volumes, but leaving the project uncompleted. He died in 1957. Social Science as a Study of Living Facts: Liminality and Van Gennep’s Project for a ‘Biological Sociology’ As can be seen, Arnold van Gennep, before being ostracized from the French social sciences, was not just a talented scholar making contributions within this or that field or topic area: he was engaged in something more than that, namely providing the social sciences with a methodology. It was also for this reason that he was a serious threat to the currents which were then establishing themselves. But what was van Gennep’s scientific project? A full answer to this question is beyond the limits of our present argument, but it is important to know that van Gennep did have a project. And the concept of liminality holds its place within the contours of that project – or what we can today reconstruct as that project in-the-making. While van Gennep was indeed ‘single-minded’ and a man of his own will, in many ways his project did share affinities with that of Durkheim and his students. Van Gennep was not one of those hubristic personalities who thought he could invent everything anew and from scratch. His temper and his whole outlook was driven by passion, humbleness, and working from the ground up, building on to all existing knowledge, piece by piece, paying the utmost attention to every little detail, and moving back and forth between those microscopic details (the texture of a carpet, the rhythm of a song, the shape of a basket) and the larger picture (the role of sexuality in the human psyche, the binary structures of folk songs, the universal pattern of rites). Van Gennep wanted to make a contribution towards the establishment of an empirical social science focused on the systematic, comparative in-depth study of material and symbolic culture among living peoples.
